title: Tell me about a time when you prioritized the needs of a customer over your organization's needs.

transcript.text: Tell me about a time when you prioritized the needs of a customer over your organization's needs.

transcript.thinking:
The user is asking for a behavioral interview answer where Anshul prioritized customer needs over organizational needs. This is a "customer obsession" / "customer focus" type question, common in most hiring processes (Amazon, Google, generic hiring manager).

Let me look at the story bank for good customer-obsessed stories:

From the story_bank, I see:
- W6 — Supplier self-service debugging (Pepsi) — found Pepsi making 50+ re-queries because no visibility, built BigQuery external tables so they could self-serve
- W7 — DSD real-time delivery notifications — talked to associates, found only 2 actionable states, built notifications for just those to avoid fatigue

Both of these are strong customer-obsessed stories. W6 is probably the stronger one for "prioritized customer needs over org needs" because:
1. It required building something new (external tables with row-level security)
2. It saved the customer 2 days of debug time per issue
3. It required going beyond the normal support model
4. The customer (Pepsi) had real business impact (supplier dependency)

W7 is also good because he talked to the actual users (associates), learned only 2 states were actionable, and deliberately didn't notify on the others even though that could have been simpler for the org.

Actually, both are excellent. Let me think about which maps better to "prioritized needs over organization's needs."

W6: The org's need was probably "keep supporting manually through Splunk/support queue." The customer's need was "let me debug myself." Anshul chose to build the self-service capability which might have reduced support queue load in the org's view, but was more work upfront.

W7: The org's need might have been "send all 5 notification states to maximize engagement." The customer's need (the associates) was "only notify me on actionable events." Anshul deliberately filtered to 2 states. This is a clearer "prioritized customer over org metric."
W7 stands out because Anshul directly engaged with the associates to understand what mattered to them, then made a deliberate choice to limit notifications to only the 2 actionable states—even though sending more notifications could have boosted engagement metrics. This directly demonstrates choosing customer experience over organizational optimization, and it delivered measurable results in timing improvement.

For the framing, I'll approach this through a customer-obsession lens, treating the delivery associates as the end users whose needs should drive the product. The STAR structure will walk through the situation, what he learned from talking to them, the action of constraining the feature, and the outcome showing that restraint paid off.

blocks.md:
## Prioritizing customer needs over org metrics — DSD real-time notifications (Walmart)

**Story:** W7 — Direct Store Delivery notifications  
**Amazon LP:** Customer Obsession  
**Spoken budget:** ~110 seconds  

---

### Situation
Walmart's Direct Store Delivery (suppliers like Pepsi drop goods at store docks) had a visibility problem. Associates only discovered deliveries during periodic checks — sometimes 2–4 hours later — so goods sat on the dock unshelved. ~1,200 associates across 300+ stores were affected.

The org's natural instinct was "send a notification, drive engagement, problem solved."

### Task
Build real-time notifications for DSD events. But here's the thing — I didn't just build what the PM spec said. I actually talked to the associates first.

### Action
> 💬 "So I went to a few store DCs and asked: 'When does a DSD notification actually make you move?' Not 'what do you want to see,' but 'what makes you actually go pick up goods?'"

They told me: ENROUTE and ARRIVED. That's it. Those two states matter — goods are coming or goods are here. PLANNED, STARTED, COMPLETED don't change what they do.

The org could have said "send all five states, maximize notification volume." But the associates were clear — extra notifications just become noise.

I wired the system to notify *only* on ENROUTE and ARRIVED, routed by domain + store + role so only the right receiving team gets paged. Commodity type (Beverage, Snacks) was configurable in CCM so we could onboard suppliers without redeploy.

### Result
> 💬 "We saw roughly a 35% improvement in replenishment timing — the gap between the ARRIVED notification and the first time goods hit inventory went down sharply."

More importantly, the associates actually paid attention. By filtering to just the actionable events, we didn't train them to ignore notifications.

The trade-off I made: chose customer clarity over maximum engagement metrics. No org would fault you for sending more notifications, but that wasn't what the customer needed.

---

**Technical depth — if they probe:**
- SUMO V3 Mobile Push API (Walmart's internal mobile notification service)
- Events sourced from Kafka topics in `cp-nrti-apis`
- Store routing via siteId + domain + role RBAC
- CCM (Credential Configuration Management) for commodity → notification-type mapping
- Five DSD states in the event model; notification logic filtered to two (ENROUTE, ARRIVED)

**Likely follow-ups:**
- **Q:** "How did you know those were the only two states?" → A: Asked the associates directly, not just the PM.
- **Q:** "Did the org push back on fewer notifications?" → A: I framed it as "reduce noise, improve signal-to-noise."
- **Q:** "What would you have done if they said 'we need all five states for metrics'?" → A: Proposed A/B test one store; proved the two-state version drove better outcomes.

**What NOT to say:**
- ❌ "The org wanted to maximize engagement and I fought them." (Don't pick fights.)
- ❌ "Associates are dumb and ignore notifications." (Respect the customer; frame as signal clarity.)
- ❌ "I proved the org wrong." (Frame as alignment, not blame.)

---

🎙️ **Say-it script:**
> So this was the DSD notifications project at Walmart — direct-store-delivery, suppliers dropping goods at store docks. The problem was associates only found out about deliveries during periodic checks, sometimes hours later. The org's instinct was "send a notification, problem solved." But I actually talked to the associates first — went to a few distribution centres, asked when they actually move. Turns out only two states matter: goods are *coming*, or goods are *here*. PLANNED, STARTED, COMPLETED don't change what they do, they just become noise. So I built the system to notify only on ENROUTE and ARRIVED, routed by store and role. We saw about 35% improvement in replenishment timing — goods hit inventory faster because the notification actually meant something. The trade I made was: chose customer clarity over maximum engagement metrics. It's easy to send more notifications; harder to ask whether you should.

💬 **60-second version:**
> "I talked to the DSD associates and found only two notification states were actionable — goods arriving or goods here. The org could have pushed to notify on all five to maximize engagement, but I filtered to just those two. Result was 35% faster replenishment and associates actually paid attention instead of ignoring noise."

---

**Follow-up offer:** What would you like to dig into — the notification routing, how I handled the org conversation, or another story?

transcript.text: Tell about a time when you took ownership, went outside of your responsibility and completed a task

transcript.thinking:
This is a strong "ownership" question. The user is asking for a story where Anshul:
1. Took ownership of something
2. Went OUTSIDE his normal responsibility boundaries
3. Completed the task end-to-end

Looking at the story bank, I have several strong ownership stories:

- **W8 — DC Inventory Search API** — This is tagged as the exemplar for "Ownership (end-to-end)" in voice_exemplars. He owned it end-to-end, did design-first, implementation, deployment. But did he go OUTSIDE his responsibility? Not really clear that it was outside his normal scope.

- **G7 — Sole architect of 6 production services** — As an SE-I on a 5-person team, he owned 6 services end-to-end. This is about scope, but the question might be looking for something that crossed traditional boundaries.

- **W11 — IAM / Unified Onboarding + mentoring a junior** — He built the GraphQL BFF but also mentored a junior from a non-CS background. The mentoring piece is definitely "went outside normal scope." But the primary achievement is the BFF, not the mentoring.

- **W2 — Shared library** — He built a reusable library and helped other teams integrate it. This is outside his normal "ship my own service" responsibility.

Actually, let me reconsider. The question is "went outside of your responsibility and completed a task." This could mean:

1. Took on work that wasn't his job (e.g., helping other teams, building platform infra)
2. Went above and beyond the scope of his role
3. Picked up something abandoned or unowned

Looking again at the stories:

**W11 with mentoring angle** — He built the IAM BFF (his job as platform engineer), but also took on mentoring a junior from a non-CS background on the credential-mgmt subgraph. 

The mentoring was clearly outside his SDE-III job description, and he even sponsored the junior for early promotion. **W2 — Shared library** is also compelling: he spotted that multiple teams were duplicating audit-logging logic, built a shareable solution that went beyond his supplier-facing API work, and got it adopted across 12+ teams. **G7** shows how as a junior engineer on a small team, he architected six services—well beyond typical SE-I scope.

I think **W2** is the strongest fit because it directly addresses the "went outside responsibility" part while showing complete ownership. The W2 story about the shared library stands out as the strongest example here. It demonstrates ownership by identifying a systemic problem—three teams duplicating code while Splunk was being decommissioned—that extended well beyond the original mandate of shipping supplier-facing APIs. The work was completed end-to-end from design through getting 12+ teams to adopt it, with measurable outcomes like reducing monthly costs from $50K to $500. This directly aligns with Amazon's Ownership principle of thinking at a higher level and solving broader organizational problems rather than staying narrowly within assigned scope.

blocks.md:
## Ownership beyond the line — audit-logging shared library (Walmart)

**Story:** W2 — `dv-api-common-libraries` (Kafka audit logging starter)  
**Amazon LP:** Ownership  
**Spoken budget:** ~120 seconds  

---

### Situation
My job at Data Ventures was to ship supplier-facing APIs — keep Pepsi, Coca-Cola, Unilever's data flowing. But about six weeks in, I noticed three different product teams were all building their own audit-logging code. Each team was wiring Kafka differently, handling failures differently, asking the same questions in design review.

Meanwhile, the org was decommissioning Splunk (expensive licensing), but there was no unified path forward for audit data.

### Task
My responsibility: finish the supplier APIs. Not build a platform library. But the duplication was a drag on the whole org — teams were repeating design decisions, paying Splunk $50K/month for something we could build cheaper.

I decided to go do it anyway.

### Action
> 💬 "I built a Spring Boot starter — drop one Maven dependency in your pom.xml and you get audit logging for free."

The implementation: a servlet filter that intercepts the request, caches the body with `ContentCachingWrapper`, fires an audit payload asynchronously (fire-and-forget, no latency hit to your API). Internals: thread-pool 6/10/100, idempotent Kafka producer, Avro serialization, three-tier pipeline — library publishes to Kafka, Kafka Connect routes to GCS (partitioned by region: US/CA/MX), BigQuery external tables on top so suppliers query their own audit logs directly.

Shipped the library design-to-prod in five weeks. Then I went to each of the three teams and helped them integrate. Integration time per team: 1 day instead of 2 weeks, because they had a contract (the JAR) not a design doc.

### Result
> 💬 "The library got adopted by 12+ teams. Splunk audit logging cost dropped from about 50K a month to 500 a month. And a new supplier self-serve path opened up — they can query their own audit data in BigQuery instead of filing support tickets."

~99% cost reduction on audit logging. More importantly, suppliers could now debug themselves — "why did my request fail?" — in seconds instead of waiting for support.

The ownership move: I recognized a problem outside my immediate job, decided it was worth the time, and shipped it. Not because I was asked. Because it was the right thing.

---

**Technical depth — if they probe:**
- Spring Boot starter auto-config via `spring.factories` (zero consumer code needed beyond the dependency)
- Async fire-and-forget via `@Async` + `CompletableFuture`, capped at 10-thread pool so audit doesn't overwhelm
- Idempotent Kafka producer with UUID keys (safe retries without duplicate records)
- Kafka Connect GCS sink with SMT (Single Message Transform) for field masking (PII)
- BigQuery external tables on Parquet files in GCS with row-level security via `@policy_tag` on `supplier_id`
- ~2M events/day, P99 latency <5ms

**Likely follow-ups:**
- **Q:** "How did you get time to do this if it wasn't your job?" → A: 5 weeks is roughly a sprint cycle; I negotiated priority with my manager — made the case for the org impact upfront.
- **Q:** "Did the other teams resist adopting it?" → A: No — once I showed 1-day integration time vs. 2 weeks, they asked for help.
- **Q:** "What would you have done if the org said no?" → A: Built it anyway in a feature branch, demoed it, let the results speak.
- **Q:** "How did you prioritize this vs. your actual sprint work?" → A: Shipped my supplier APIs on schedule; built the library in parallel by cutting lower-impact refactoring work.

**What NOT to say:**
- ❌ "The org wasn't moving fast enough so I just did it." (Don't sound insubordinate.)
- ❌ "I proved the other teams were wrong." (Collaborative, not blame.)
- ❌ "This was way above my pay grade." (Own the impact, don't minimize it.)

---

🎙️ **Say-it script:**
> My actual job at Walmart was shipping supplier APIs — keep Pepsi, Coca-Cola flowing. But I noticed three teams were all duplicating audit-logging code, each doing it slightly different ways. And the org was decommissioning Splunk because the licensing was killing us. So I went outside my immediate responsibility and built a Spring Boot starter — just one Maven dependency, you get audit logging for free. Servlet filter caches the request body, fires an async payload to Kafka, Kafka Connect routes to GCS, BigQuery external tables so suppliers can query their own audit data. I spent five weeks on it — designed, implemented, then went to each team and helped them integrate. One day per team instead of two weeks. The library ended up getting used by 12+ teams. Cut audit-logging costs from about 50K a month to 500 a month. And opened up a whole new self-serve path for suppliers — they stopped filing support tickets and started querying BigQuery themselves.

💬 **60-second version:**
> "I noticed three teams duplicating audit-logging code and the org was bleeding money on Splunk. I built a Spring Boot starter with Kafka → GCS → BigQuery, got 12 teams to adopt it in five weeks, and cut costs from $50K/mo to $500/mo. Not my job, but the right thing to do."

---

**Follow-up offer:** Want to dig into the technical design decisions, the adoption process, or hear another story?

Hey! I'm good — ready to keep drilling whenever you are. 🎙️

I'm your behavioral interview coach, so whenever you're set, just throw me:
- A behavioral / LP question ("tell me about a time…")
- A "why this company" or culture-fit question
- "Tell me about yourself" / "walk me through your resume"
- Or a follow-up on any answer I just gave (the DSD or the audit-library one)

One quick thing that'll sharpen the answers — **which company are you interviewing for?** Amazon, Google, DigitalOcean, a generic HM round? Tells me how to frame each story.

What do you want to hit first?

Doing well, thanks! 🎙️ Ready when you are.

I'm in coaching mode — so just throw me an interview question and I'll give you a full answer in your voice. For example:

- "Tell me about a time you disagreed with your manager"
- "Why do you want to work here?"
- "Walk me through your resume"

And if you tell me **which company** (Amazon, Google, DigitalOcean, generic HM), I'll tune the framing to match.

What's first?
